explanations.b: Port security limits MAC addresses per port but does not control which VLANs a device can initiate connections to.
explanations.c: ACLs on the inter-VLAN routing interface enforce directional traffic policies; permit rules allow VLAN 10 to reach VLAN 99 cameras while deny rules block all traffic originating from VLAN 99; stateful firewalls can permit only established sessions. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: Private VLANs restrict communication between ports within a VLAN not between VLANs; configuring PVLANs on VLAN 10 would isolate corporate devices from each other which is opposite to the requirement.

questions.text: What is a Switch Virtual Interface (SVI) and how does it differ from a physical switch interface?

answers.text: An SVI is a physical port on a Layer 3 switch converted to a routed port; when an access port is configured as an SVI it can no longer carry VLAN traffic
An SVI is a secondary virtual interface that duplicates a physical port for redundancy; each physical interface has one corresponding SVI that activates if the physical port fails
An SVI is the management interface used exclusively for SNMP polling; SVIs accept only SNMP queries and cannot carry data traffic

An SVI is a virtual Layer 3 interface associated with a VLAN on a Layer 3 switch; it provides a gateway IP address for the VLAN enabling inter-VLAN routing within the switch itself; it also provides a management IP address for remote switch access via SSH or Telnet

explanations.a: SVIs are not converted physical ports; they are separate virtual logical interfaces that coexist with physical ports; routed ports (no switchport) are a different concept.
explanations.b: SVIs have no redundancy relationship with physical ports; SVI availability depends on whether at least one physical port in the associated VLAN is operational.
explanations.c: SVIs carry all types of IP traffic not just SNMP; they are full Layer 3 interfaces that route user data traffic, management traffic, and any other IP protocol between VLANs.
explanations.d: SVIs are virtual Layer 3 interfaces bound to VLANs; the command interface vlan 10 creates an SVI serving as the default gateway for VLAN 10; the management SVI provides IP connectivity for remote switch administration. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: A Layer 3 switch has SVIs for VLAN 10 (10.10.0.1/24) and VLAN 20 (10.20.0.1/24). A host in VLAN 10 cannot reach a server in VLAN 20. Both SVIs are up and the host has the correct default gateway. What is the most likely missing configuration?

answers.text: IP routing must be enabled with the ip routing command; Layer 3 switches have routing disabled by default; without ip routing the SVIs have IP addresses but the switch does not perform inter-VLAN routing
The switch needs static routes pointing each VLAN to its SVI; Layer 3 switches require manual static routes for all directly connected VLANs before routing between them
VTP must be in server mode before SVIs can route between VLANs; SVI routing requires VTP to advertise the VLAN database to the routing engine
OSPF must be enabled on both SVIs; Layer 3 switches require a dynamic routing protocol to route between directly connected VLANs

explanations.a: IP routing is disabled by default on Cisco Layer 3 switches; the switch operates as a Layer 2 device until ip routing is explicitly enabled in global configuration; with this command the hardware routing engine activates and the switch begins forwarding packets between SVIs. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: Static routes are not needed for directly connected networks; once ip routing is enabled the switch automatically installs connected routes for all SVI subnets.
explanations.c: VTP manages VLAN database propagation between switches and has no effect on routing engine activation; VTP mode does not need to be server for SVIs to route.
explanations.d: Dynamic routing protocols are not required for directly connected VLAN routing; once ip routing is enabled connected routes for all SVI subnets are automatically installed.

questions.text: An SVI for VLAN 100 on a Layer 3 switch shows line protocol is down even though VLAN 100 exists in the VLAN database. What conditions must be met for the SVI to come up?

answers.text: The SVI requires a physical loopback cable connected to a VLAN 100 port; Layer 3 switches need a physical link to validate SVI configuration
IP routing must be enabled before the SVI shows line protocol up; enabling ip routing activates all SVIs simultaneously

The VLAN must exist in the VLAN database and at least one physical access port assigned to that VLAN must be up/up OR at least one trunk port carrying that VLAN must be up/up and the SVI must not be administratively shut down; if no active ports carry VLAN 100 traffic the SVI remains line protocol down
SVIs come up automatically when the first device connects to any switch port; the SVI monitors DHCP requests and activates when a valid device joins the VLAN

explanations.a: SVIs do not require physical loopback cables; they come up based on the status of physical ports in the associated VLAN.
explanations.b: IP routing activation does not determine SVI line protocol state; the SVI state is determined by active Layer 2 membership in the VLAN.
explanations.c: SVI state depends on physical Layer 2 activity in the associated VLAN; the SVI represents the Layer 3 gateway for a Layer 2 VLAN; if no ports are up carrying VLAN 100 there is no active Layer 2 segment so the SVI line protocol remains down. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: SVIs do not monitor DHCP requests to determine activation; SVI line protocol status is based on port state in the associated VLAN not on DHCP activity.

questions.text: A network administrator needs to remotely manage a Cisco Layer 2-only switch via SSH. How is the management IP configured?

answers.text: Configure an IP address directly on any physical port; Layer 2 switches support IP addressing on physical ports for management without requiring SVIs
Configure a management IP only through the console port; SSH management of Layer 2 switches is not supported without IP routing enabled

Configure an IP address on the management SVI such as interface vlan 1 or a dedicated management VLAN SVI; even a Layer 2-only switch uses the SVI on the management VLAN for SSH/Telnet/SNMP access; the default gateway must also be configured for off-subnet management access; no ip routing is needed for management-only SVI use
Layer 2 switches cannot have IP addresses; they operate entirely at Layer 2 and management requires direct console connection

explanations.a: Physical switch ports on Layer 2 switches do not support IP addressing; they operate as Layer 2 switchports; the management IP is placed on an SVI.
explanations.b: SSH is fully supported on Layer 2 switches through the management SVI IP address.
explanations.c: Management SVI is the standard mechanism for switch IP addressing; every Cisco switch even Layer 2-only can have an SVI on the management VLAN for SSH/Telnet/SNMP access; the switch needs a default gateway to reach management stations on other subnets; ip routing is NOT required for management-only SVI use. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: Layer 2 switches absolutely support IP addresses through SVIs; the management VLAN SVI provides the IP stack for remote management.

questions.text: What is the native VLAN on a trunk port and what type of traffic does it carry?

answers.text: The native VLAN is the highest-numbered VLAN on a trunk port; all VLANs with numbers above 4000 are native and carry management traffic between switches

The native VLAN on a trunk port carries untagged traffic; frames sent without an 802.1Q VLAN tag on a trunk port are assumed to belong to the native VLAN; the default native VLAN on Cisco switches is VLAN 1 and the native VLAN must match on both ends of a trunk
The native VLAN is the VLAN used exclusively for spanning tree BPDUs; STP frames travel on the native VLAN because they are too large to fit in a standard 802.1Q tagged frame
The native VLAN is dynamically assigned based on which device connects to the trunk port first

explanations.a: The native VLAN is not determined by highest VLAN number; it is a configured setting (default VLAN 1) that specifies which VLAN receives untagged traffic on a trunk.
explanations.b: Native VLAN handles untagged trunk traffic; 802.1Q tagging is optional for one VLAN per trunk; the native VLAN's frames are sent without tags; native VLAN mismatch between trunk endpoints causes traffic to be placed in the wrong VLAN on the receiving switch. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: STP BPDUs are sent as untagged frames on the native VLAN but the native VLAN's purpose is not exclusively for STP; BPDUs are not too large for 802.1Q frames.
explanations.d: Native VLAN is a static configured parameter not dynamically assigned based on connected devices.

questions.text: A port connects an IP phone with a PC daisy-chained to it. Voice traffic should use VLAN 10 and data traffic should use VLAN 20. How is this dual-VLAN configuration achieved on a single access port?

answers.text: Configure the port as a trunk port allowing VLANs 10 and 20; trunk ports carry multiple VLANs and the IP phone and PC are assigned to their respective VLANs using MAC address filtering
Configure two physical cables from the switch; one cable to the IP phone (VLAN 10) and one directly to the PC (VLAN 20); a single port cannot carry voice and data simultaneously

Configure the port with switchport access vlan 20 for the data VLAN and switchport voice vlan 10 for voice; the voice VLAN feature allows a single access port to carry both untagged data traffic (VLAN 20) and 802.1p-tagged voice traffic (VLAN 10); the IP phone processes voice VLAN membership via CDP/LLDP and tags its voice frames; the PC sends untagged frames assigned to the data VLAN
Configure LLDP-MED only; LLDP-MED automatically negotiates VLAN assignments between switch and phone without any VLAN configuration commands

explanations.a: Trunk ports are not typically used for endpoint connections; voice VLAN specifically solves this dual-VLAN requirement on access ports without making them full trunks.
explanations.b: Two physical cables are not required; voice VLAN specifically addresses the single-port dual-VLAN requirement for VoIP deployments.
explanations.c: Voice VLAN is the purpose-built feature for this scenario; Cisco switchport voice vlan enables a second VLAN for VoIP traffic on an access port; the switch advertises the voice VLAN to the IP phone via CDP or LLDP-MED; this enables QoS prioritization of voice traffic. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: LLDP-MED advertises network policy including voice VLAN to endpoints but the switch must still have the voice VLAN configured; without configured voice VLAN there is nothing to advertise.

questions.text: A security auditor flags that the native VLAN is VLAN 1 (the default) on all trunk ports. What specific security risk does this create and what is the recommended remediation?

answers.text: VLAN 1 as native VLAN creates a performance risk; untagged management traffic on VLAN 1 competes with user data traffic; the fix is increasing trunk port speed
VLAN 1 as native VLAN prevents trunk negotiation; DTP cannot negotiate trunk mode when native VLAN is VLAN 1
VLAN 1 native VLAN creates a broadcast storm; STP BPDUs on VLAN 1 loop indefinitely because Spanning Tree cannot operate on the native VLAN

VLAN 1 as native VLAN enables VLAN hopping via double-tagging attacks; an attacker on a VLAN 1 access port sends frames with two 802.1Q tags: outer tag VLAN 1 and inner tag for the target VLAN; the first switch strips the native VLAN tag and forwards the frame with the inner tag; the second switch delivers it to the target VLAN; the fix is changing native VLAN on all trunk ports to an unused VLAN not assigned to any access port

explanations.a: VLAN 1 as native VLAN is a security risk not primarily a performance concern.
explanations.b: Native VLAN does not affect DTP trunk negotiation; DTP is controlled by port mode configuration.
explanations.c: Spanning Tree operates correctly with VLAN 1 as native VLAN; the native VLAN does not cause STP loops.
explanations.d: Double-tagging VLAN hopping is the specific attack enabled by matching native VLANs; by using an unused native VLAN that attackers cannot access the double-tagging attack is mitigated. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: What does 802.1Q VLAN tagging add to an Ethernet frame and why is it used on trunk ports?

answers.text: 802.1Q adds a 64-byte encryption header to Ethernet frames protecting VLAN membership information from unauthorized switches

802.1Q inserts a 4-byte tag into the Ethernet frame header containing a 12-bit VLAN ID supporting up to 4094 VLANs and a 3-bit priority field for QoS; trunk ports use 802.1Q tagging so a single physical link can carry frames from multiple VLANs; each frame is tagged with its source VLAN ID allowing the receiving switch to place it in the correct VLAN
802.1Q adds a 2-byte VLAN number to the IP header between source and destination address fields; routers read this field to make VLAN-aware routing decisions
802.1Q adds a sequence number to Ethernet frames to prevent out-of-order delivery on trunk ports

explanations.a: 802.1Q does not encrypt frames; it is a VLAN identification mechanism; the tag is readable by any 802.1Q-aware switch.
explanations.b: The 802.1Q tag is a 4-byte insertion between the source MAC address and EtherType fields; it contains the Tag Protocol Identifier 0x8100, 3-bit Priority Code Point, 1-bit Drop Eligible Indicator, and 12-bit VLAN ID; trunk ports tag frames allowing multiple VLANs to share one physical link. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: VLAN tags are added at Layer 2 in the Ethernet frame not in the IP header; 802.1Q operates at the Data Link layer; IP headers have no VLAN tag fields.
explanations.d: 802.1Q does not add sequence numbers for frame ordering; sequence numbers are a transport layer TCP concern.

questions.text: A trunk port is configured with 'switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30'. VLAN 40 is added to the switch but the engineer forgets to update the trunk. VLAN 40 users on this switch cannot reach VLAN 40 users on the next switch. What is the cause and fix?

answers.text: VLAN 40 traffic is not permitted on the trunk; the allowed VLAN list explicitly excludes VLAN 40; tagged frames for VLAN 40 arriving at the trunk port are dropped; the fix is adding VLAN 40 with switchport trunk allowed vlan add 40 (not overwriting the entire list)
The trunk automatically blocks new VLANs for security; all new VLANs are in a blocked state by default requiring manual allow after creation
The trunk requires rebooting after adding new VLANs; the trunk port caches its allowed VLAN list at boot time
VLAN 40 is blocked because it conflicts with the switch's internal spanning tree instance

explanations.a: Explicit trunk allowed VLAN lists require updates when new VLANs are added; the add keyword is critical: switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30,40 replaces the entire list while switchport trunk allowed vlan add 40 appends to the existing list; forgetting add removes existing VLANs. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: VLANs are not automatically blocked for security; the allowed VLAN list explicitly includes permitted VLANs; absent VLANs are excluded not blocked as a security feature.
explanations.c: Trunk port allowed VLAN changes take effect immediately without rebooting.
explanations.d: Spanning Tree does not block VLANs based on how many VLANs exist on a trunk; STP makes forwarding decisions based on topology not VLAN count.

questions.text: An access port in VLAN 10 is receiving tagged frames with VLAN 10 from a neighboring device. The switch drops these frames. What can be configured to change this behavior and what are the security implications?

answers.text: Enable 802.1Q pass-through mode; this mode forwards all tagged frames regardless of VLAN membership and is recommended for all access ports
Configure the switchport access vlan tag command; this Cisco-proprietary command accepts pre-tagged frames for the configured access VLAN without changing to trunk mode

Configure the port as a trunk port; this allows it to receive and process tagged frames; however this changes the port's behavior entirely and may allow more VLANs than intended creating potential security exposure if not combined with appropriate allowed VLAN restrictions
Configure 802.1ad QinQ; the device sending tagged frames must be encapsulated in an outer service tag before the access port can process the inner VLAN 10 tag

explanations.a: There is no standard 802.1Q pass-through mode for access ports; access ports drop incoming tagged frames by design as an intentional security behavior.
explanations.b: There is no standard switchport access vlan tag command on Cisco IOS; this command does not exist.
explanations.c: Converting the port to a trunk is the primary mechanism for handling tagged frames; a trunk port processes 802.1Q-tagged frames appropriately; restricting the trunk to only VLAN 10 limits VLAN exposure; converting access ports to trunks increases attack surface and should be done deliberately. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: 802.1ad QinQ encapsulates customer VLAN tags within provider tags for service provider environments; it is not the solution for making an access port accept customer tagged frames.

questions.text: What is link aggregation and what does LACP specifically provide?

answers.text: Link aggregation combines multiple physical links into one logical link to increase bandwidth and provide redundancy; LACP is the IEEE 802.3ad standard that dynamically negotiates and manages link aggregation between two devices automatically detecting and recovering from individual link failures
Link aggregation is a Layer 3 protocol that combines multiple routing paths for WAN load balancing; LACP negotiates which routing protocol manages the aggregated WAN paths
Link aggregation encrypts multiple parallel links using LACP keys; each link uses a different encryption key derived from LACP negotiation
Link aggregation is the same as ECMP routing; LACP distributes packets across multiple Layer 3 paths using a hashing algorithm

explanations.a: Link aggregation bundles multiple physical Ethernet links into one logical interface; bandwidth increases proportionally (2x1G = 2G aggregate); if one link fails traffic continues on remaining links; LACP provides standardized dynamic negotiation detecting partner capabilities and managing member link status automatically. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: Link aggregation operates at Layer 2 between directly connected devices not WAN routing paths; OSPF and EIGRP are not managed by LACP.
explanations.c: LACP does not provide encryption; it is a control protocol for managing physical link membership; LACP keys are used for PDU authentication not data encryption.
explanations.d: Link aggregation and ECMP routing are different technologies; ECMP distributes Layer 3 routed traffic; LACP aggregates Layer 2 physical links.

questions.text: A network engineer configures an EtherChannel using LACP with one switch set to 'channel-group 1 mode active' and the other to 'channel-group 1 mode on'. The port channel does not form. Why?

answers.text: The channel group numbers must differ across both switches; using the same group number on both sides creates a conflict
LACP active mode requires Gigabit interfaces; Fast Ethernet interfaces only support LACP passive mode

Mode on and LACP active are incompatible; mode on forces the port channel without negotiation while LACP active sends LACP PDUs and expects LACP responses; a static on port does not send or respond to LACP PDUs so negotiation fails; both sides must use LACP (active/active or active/passive) or both must use static on mode
EtherChannel group number 1 is reserved for spanning tree; using group 1 for LACP creates a conflict with spanning tree root bridge election

explanations.a: Channel group numbers are locally significant and do not need to match between switches; both sides using group 1 or different numbers is equally valid.
explanations.b: LACP modes are independent of interface speed; LACP works on Fast Ethernet, Gigabit, 10G, and higher speed interfaces.
explanations.c: Protocol incompatibility between static on and LACP negotiation is the cause; LACP active sends PDUs expecting LACP response; static on does not participate in LACP negotiation at all; for LACP to work both sides must run LACP or both must be on; mixing modes prevents port channel formation. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: EtherChannel group numbers have no relationship to spanning tree instances; group numbers 1-48 are available for EtherChannel configuration with no reserved range for STP.

questions.text: An EtherChannel with 4 physical links carries traffic for 10 VLANs. VLAN 5 traffic always uses the same physical link even during congestion while VLANs 1-4 and 6-10 are balanced across all four links. What explains this and how can it be changed?

answers.text: VLAN 5 is blocked by spanning tree on three of the four physical links; STP places all but one link in blocking state per VLAN
VLAN 5 is pinned to one link because it was the first VLAN active when the EtherChannel formed; EtherChannel uses first-come-first-assigned link selection
The problem is a duplex mismatch on the three unused links for VLAN 5; links with duplex mismatches only carry unicast traffic for VLANs originated before the mismatch

EtherChannel load balancing uses a hash algorithm based on configurable fields such as source MAC, destination MAC, source IP, or destination IP; all traffic matching the same hash value uses the same physical link; VLAN 5 traffic's specific addresses consistently hash to the same link creating an imbalance; changing the load-balance method with port-channel load-balance changes the hash inputs and may distribute VLAN 5 across multiple links; per-flow consistency means individual flows always use one link but different flows distribute across links

explanations.a: Spanning Tree does not block individual physical links within an EtherChannel; once the EtherChannel forms as a logical interface STP treats it as one link.
explanations.b: EtherChannel does not assign VLANs to links based on creation time; the hash algorithm determines link selection on a per-flow basis.
explanations.c: Duplex mismatches cause CRC errors and reduced performance but do not selectively restrict specific VLANs to specific links.
explanations.d: EtherChannel hashing is the correct explanation; consistent source/destination address combinations for VLAN 5 traffic consistently hash to the same link; changing the hash method changes which fields are hashed and may better distribute VLAN 5 traffic. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: What is a duplex mismatch on a switch port and what symptoms does it cause?

answers.text: A duplex mismatch occurs when two ports use different VLAN assignments; port A in VLAN 10 connected to a device expecting VLAN 20 creates a duplex mismatch

A duplex mismatch occurs when one side of a link operates in full-duplex and the other in half-duplex; the full-duplex side transmits continuously without checking for collisions; the half-duplex side uses CSMA/CD and detects collisions when the full-duplex side transmits during its own transmission; symptoms include late collisions, increased CRC errors, poor throughput, and intermittent connectivity despite the link showing as up
A duplex mismatch is when a switch port is configured for 1 Gbps but the connected device is capable of 10 Gbps; the speed negotiation failure causes only duplex to function at the lower speed
A duplex mismatch occurs when ports on different switches use different spanning tree versions; one switch using STP and another using RSTP creates a duplex conflict

explanations.a: VLAN mismatches are separate from duplex mismatches; duplex refers to whether communication is one-directional-at-a-time or bidirectional-simultaneously.
explanations.b: Duplex mismatch is one of the most common network connectivity problems; it often occurs when auto-negotiation fails and one side is manual full-duplex while the other auto-negotiates to half-duplex; the half-duplex side detects collisions causing CSMA/CD backoff and late collision errors; throughput degrades significantly while the link remains up. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Speed mismatch and duplex mismatch are different concepts; speed refers to bits per second; duplex refers to simultaneous bidirectional communication capability.
explanations.d: STP version differences are not called duplex mismatches; STP and RSTP interoperate as a separate protocol consideration from full/half duplex settings.

questions.text: A server NIC is set to auto-negotiate and the switch port is manually configured for 1000 Mbps full-duplex. The link comes up at 1000 Mbps but the server experiences poor performance with high error counts. What is the problem?

answers.text: 1000 Mbps interfaces require manual speed configuration on both ends; Gigabit Ethernet does not support auto-negotiation

The manually configured switch port and auto-negotiating server NIC create a duplex mismatch; when a port is manually configured the auto-negotiating partner does not receive auto-negotiation signals; the auto-negotiating server may fall back to half-duplex causing late collisions and errors; both sides should use auto-negotiation or both should be manually configured identically
The switch port must be configured for 100 Mbps to maintain compatibility with the server's auto-negotiation; 1000 Mbps is too fast for auto-negotiation
The issue is MTU mismatch; the switch defaults to 1500 byte MTU while the server's auto-negotiation results in 9000 byte MTU

explanations.a: Gigabit Ethernet (1000BASE-T) actually requires auto-negotiation per IEEE 802.3ab; however mixed manual/auto configuration causes the described duplex mismatch problem.
explanations.b: Mixed manual/auto configuration is a well-known best-practice violation; the IEEE recommendation is that either both ends use auto-negotiation or both are manually configured to the same speed and duplex; when one side is manual and the other is auto the auto side may not correctly determine duplex resulting in a half/full duplex mismatch. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Auto-negotiation supports Gigabit Ethernet; 802.3ab includes Gigabit Ethernet in the auto-negotiation specification.
explanations.d: Auto-negotiation does not negotiate MTU; MTU is a separate configuration parameter not exchanged via auto-negotiation.

questions.text: A storage administrator enables jumbo frames (9000 byte MTU) on a dedicated iSCSI network. Some servers achieve 9000 byte frames but others fall back to 1500 bytes even though their NICs are configured for jumbo frames. What is the cause and what diagnostic steps identify the problem?

answers.text: Jumbo frames require fiber optic cabling; copper Gigabit Ethernet is limited to 1500 byte MTU by the 802.3 standard
Jumbo frames cannot be configured on servers with auto-negotiated links; manual speed and duplex configuration is required before the NIC driver enables jumbo frame support

The problem is TCP window scaling; servers that did not negotiate TCP window scaling cannot utilize jumbo frames; the MTU configuration is correct but the TCP stack limits frame size when window scaling is disabled

All devices in the path must support the same MTU; a single device with a lower MTU creates a bottleneck; the problematic servers likely traverse a switch or port with the default 1500 byte MTU; systematic verification uses ping with large packet size and DF bit set from each server; a failed large ping reveals where MTU drops; check every switch port in the path with show interface to verify MTU; also verify all VLANs and trunk ports have jumbo frame support enabled at the switch level

explanations.a: Jumbo frames work on both copper and fiber Ethernet; 9000 byte MTU is a configuration parameter at the Ethernet MAC layer not limited by physical medium type.
explanations.b: Auto-negotiation does not limit MTU; jumbo frame support is enabled in the NIC driver, OS networking stack, and switch configuration independently of speed/duplex negotiation method.
explanations.c: TCP window scaling affects TCP receive buffer sizing not MTU; these are separate parameters.
explanations.d: Path MTU consistency is required for jumbo frames to function end-to-end; each hop must support the same MTU; the ping with DF bit set and large packet size is the standard Path MTU Discovery diagnostic tool. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: What problem does Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) solve in switched networks?

answers.text: STP prevents broadcast storms caused by Layer 2 loops; when multiple physical paths exist between switches frames without TTL circulate endlessly; STP detects the loop topology using BPDUs and selectively blocks redundant ports to create a loop-free tree topology while maintaining physical redundant links for failover
STP solves IP address conflicts; when two devices have the same IP address STP detects the duplication through BPDU exchanges and automatically reassigns IP addresses
STP solves the problem of two switches having the same MAC address; when two switches are connected their MAC addresses conflict in the CAM table and STP arbitrates which MAC is valid
STP provides load balancing across multiple physical paths between switches distributing traffic evenly to maximize bandwidth utilization

explanations.a: Layer 2 broadcast storms are the core problem STP addresses; Ethernet frames have no TTL equivalent; in a loop a broadcast frame multiplies exponentially consuming all bandwidth within seconds; STP's tree algorithm blocks redundant paths while keeping them connected for failover. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: STP does not detect or resolve IP address conflicts; IP addressing is a Layer 3 concern; STP operates at Layer 2 with no awareness of IP addresses.
explanations.c: Switches have unique MAC addresses and MAC address conflicts between switches do not occur in practice; STP uses bridge IDs based on MAC addresses for root bridge election but this is not about resolving MAC conflicts.
explanations.d: STP explicitly prevents load balancing; only one path between any two switches is active at a time; redundant paths are in blocking state.

questions.text: A new switch added to the production network has a lower bridge priority than the existing root bridge. Moments after connection, users report network outages across the building. What happened and how should this be prevented?

answers.text: The new switch became the root bridge causing spanning tree topology reconvergence; all switches recalculate their port roles relative to the new root; during convergence (30-50 seconds with classic STP) ports cycle through listening and learning states; this disrupts all Layer 2 traffic; prevention involves configuring spanning-tree vlan X priority 0 on designated core switches and spanning-tree portfast bpduguard enable on edge ports
The new switch caused a broadcast storm; new switches have full duplex disabled by default causing collisions that generate excessive broadcast traffic
The new switch reset all MAC address tables in the network; adding a new switch clears CAM tables on all connected switches causing flooding for approximately 5 seconds
The new switch blocked all trunk ports using DTP; Dynamic Trunking Protocol on the new switch overrides existing trunk configurations placing all inter-switch links in access mode

explanations.a: Unintended root bridge election is one of the most common STP-related network outages; the new switch's lower bridge ID won the root bridge election; all switches then recalculate shortest paths to the new root; during STP reconvergence ports pass through listening and learning states; proper STP design mandates setting explicit priorities on infrastructure switches and using BPDU guard on edge ports. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: Broadcast storms in this scenario are a symptom of STP reconvergence flooding not a full-duplex issue.
explanations.c: Adding a new switch does not globally clear MAC address tables on other switches; each switch independently maintains its own CAM table.
explanations.d: DTP negotiation affects individual ports not network-wide topology; even if a trunk became an access port it would affect only that specific link not cause a building-wide outage.

questions.text: What is Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol (RSTP, 802.1w) and how does it improve upon original STP (802.1D)?

answers.text: RSTP improves STP by adding load balancing; RSTP allows all redundant paths to carry traffic simultaneously eliminating port blocking entirely
RSTP supports VLANs; original STP (802.1D) only handles a single spanning tree for all VLANs while RSTP supports one spanning tree instance per VLAN
RSTP replaces BPDUs with OSPF LSAs for topology exchange; using LSAs allows RSTP to leverage the OSPF database for faster convergence

RSTP dramatically reduces convergence time from 30-50 seconds to 1-2 seconds; STP waits through 15-second listening and 15-second learning states; RSTP replaces these with a negotiation mechanism where switches actively agree on port roles; RSTP defines new port states (discarding, learning, forwarding) and new port roles (alternate and backup ports) that can transition rapidly to forwarding; edge ports with PortFast immediately enter forwarding state

explanations.a: RSTP still blocks redundant paths like STP; the improvement is convergence speed not simultaneous multi-path forwarding.
explanations.b: Per-VLAN spanning tree is Cisco's PVST+ extension; IEEE 802.1w (RSTP) still defines one instance for all VLANs; MSTP (802.1s) is the IEEE standard for multiple instances.
explanations.c: RSTP uses BPDUs not OSPF LSAs; OSPF is a Layer 3 routing protocol entirely separate from Layer 2 spanning tree.
explanations.d: Convergence speed is RSTP's primary improvement; STP's 30-50 second convergence is unacceptable for modern networks; RSTP uses active handshaking (proposal/agreement) to instantly transition ports to forwarding when topology changes. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: A network uses Rapid PVST+ with VLANs 1-100. The architect wants Switch A as root for VLANs 1-50 and Switch B as root for VLANs 51-100. What STP concept enables this and what are the risks?

answers.text: Configure STP port priorities; setting different port priorities on Switch A and Switch B determines which switch handles which VLANs
Configure PortFast on all ports; PortFast enables all switch ports to immediately enter forwarding state and the switch automatically elects itself as root for VLANs matching its port number parity
Configure BPDU guard on both switches; BPDU guard prevents external switches from becoming root bridge; enabling it on Switch A blocks Switch B from participating in root bridge election for any VLAN

Configure per-VLAN STP bridge priorities; Rapid PVST+ runs a separate spanning tree instance per VLAN; configuring Switch A with priority 4096 for VLANs 1-50 and Switch B with priority 4096 for VLANs 51-100 makes each switch the root for its VLAN set; ports on Switch B blocking for VLANs 1-50 are forwarding for VLANs 51-100 enabling traffic engineering; risks include asymmetric topology if configurations are mismatched, increased complexity managing 100 STP instances, and suboptimal paths if VLAN-to-root assignments are not aligned with traffic patterns

explanations.a: Port priorities influence designated port selection on shared segments not root bridge election for all VLANs; bridge priority controls root bridge election.
explanations.b: PortFast and BPDU guard are edge port features not root bridge election tools; PortFast does not create VLAN affinity based on port parity.
explanations.c: BPDU guard disables a port when a BPDU is received; it is designed to protect edge ports from unauthorized switches; enabling it on inter-switch links would shut down those ports breaking inter-switch connectivity.
explanations.d: Per-VLAN bridge priority configuration enables load balancing across STP instances; Rapid PVST+ maintains separate spanning trees per VLAN; different root bridges for different VLAN groups cause different ports to be blocking or forwarding per VLAN effectively load-balancing traffic across redundant switch links. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: A switch shows 4 active LACP member ports in a port channel but the server shows only 2 active and 2 standby. What determines which ports are active versus standby in an LACP port channel?

answers.text: LACP randomly selects active ports from available member links at each connection establishment; running lacp reset forces a new random selection

LACP limits active member ports to the number the lower-capability partner supports; if the switch supports 8 LACP members but the server NIC driver supports only 2 active ports LACP uses the minimum; LACP selects active ports based on port priority (lower number preferred) and hot-standby ports activate automatically when an active port fails
The 2 standby ports are experiencing duplex mismatches; LACP places mismatched ports in standby mode until speed and duplex are corrected
LACP requires equal numbers of active ports on both sides; since the switch shows 4 active but the server shows 2 the configuration is invalid and the switch must be reconfigured to 2 active ports

explanations.a: LACP does not use random port selection; it uses a deterministic algorithm based on port priority (configured value) and port number; the selection is consistent after configuration changes.
explanations.b: LACP hot-standby keeps additional member ports connected but not actively forwarding; the maximum active links is limited by the lesser of what each side supports; many network adapters support 2-active-2-standby configurations; port priority values determine which ports are active and which are hot-standby; when an active port fails a standby activates immediately. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Duplex mismatches cause errors but do not place LACP ports in standby mode; a duplex-mismatched port would cause performance issues but LACP has no mechanism to detect and isolate duplex mismatches by placing ports in standby.
explanations.d: LACP does not require equal numbers of active ports on both sides; the number of active ports is independently determined by each side's configuration and capabilities.

questions.text: A switch port shows input errors and CRC errors increasing steadily. The link shows as up at 1 Gbps full-duplex. What are the most likely causes?

answers.text: CRC errors always indicate a software bug in the switch OS; hardware-generated CRC checks cannot produce errors on a correctly functioning physical connection

CRC errors on a 1 Gbps link indicate that the link is actually operating at 100 Mbps; the interface display showing 1 Gbps is a misreport; CRC errors only appear when negotiated speed differs from actual transmission speed
CRC errors indicate excessive broadcast traffic; broadcasts from multiple VLANs create frame collisions at the switch buffer detected as CRC errors

CRC errors indicate frames arriving with corrupted data that fail the cyclic redundancy check; on a link showing as up at 1 Gbps the most common causes are: a faulty Ethernet cable (damaged conductor, poor termination, bend radius violation), a bad connector or damaged SFP transceiver, a duplex mismatch causing collisions that produce malformed frames, EMI interference corrupting the signal, or a failing NIC; systematic diagnosis involves replacing the cable, checking connector condition, verifying duplex on both ends, and testing with a cable tester

explanations.a: CRC errors are physical and data link layer issues not OS bugs; the CRC is computed in hardware from the frame contents; if the computed CRC does not match the transmitted CRC the frame is discarded and counted as an error.
explanations.b: CRC errors are independent of whether the reported speed matches actual speed; CRC errors indicate data corruption not speed mismatch.
explanations.c: Broadcast traffic does not cause CRC errors; broadcasts are frames delivered to all devices in a broadcast domain; CRC errors are signal integrity issues at the physical layer.
explanations.d: CRC errors on active links indicate signal or frame integrity problems; physical causes (cable damage, bad connectors, dirty fiber connectors, bent cables) introduce signal noise that causes bit errors; a duplex mismatch causes the half-duplex side to corrupt frames; EMI from adjacent power cables can induce noise; systematic physical layer troubleshooting resolves most CRC error causes. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: A switch shows VLAN 50 as active but users on VLAN 50 cannot communicate with the default gateway (SVI for VLAN 50 on a different Layer 3 switch). What must exist between the two switches for VLAN 50 to function end-to-end?

answers.text: A DHCP reservation on the VLAN 50 SVI; the Layer 3 switch must have a static DHCP reservation for each VLAN 50 device before the SVI can respond to gateway requests

A trunk link between the two switches with VLAN 50 in the allowed VLAN list; VLAN 50 traffic from the access switch must be tagged with VLAN 50 and traverse a trunk to reach the Layer 3 switch where the SVI is configured; if the trunk does not allow VLAN 50 the tagged frames are dropped and never reach the SVI
A static route on the access switch pointing to the VLAN 50 SVI IP address; Layer 2 access switches require manual static routes for each VLAN SVI
A spanning tree PortFast configuration on VLAN 50 ports; without PortFast VLAN 50 frames remain in STP blocking state and cannot reach the SVI

explanations.a: DHCP reservations are for IP address assignment to specific devices and are unrelated to whether the SVI is reachable.
explanations.b: An 802.1Q trunk link carrying VLAN 50 is the required connectivity; the access switch VLAN 50 frames must be tagged and transported across the trunk to the Layer 3 switch; if the trunk blocks VLAN 50 the SVI is unreachable. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Layer 2 access switches do not have routing capability and cannot use static routes; they forward frames based on MAC addresses not IP routes.
explanations.d: PortFast affects how quickly an edge port enters forwarding state for end-device ports; it does not affect inter-switch VLAN reachability.

questions.text: A network engineer configures spanning-tree portfast on a port connecting to a PC. What does PortFast do, and what is the risk if PortFast is accidentally configured on a port connected to another switch?

answers.text: PortFast increases the STP port priority to 0 making the port the designated port for its segment; accidentally applying PortFast to a switch-to-switch link overrides normal root bridge election

PortFast disables all BPDU transmission on the configured port; configuring PortFast on a switch-to-switch link causes the port to stop sending BPDUs preventing the remote switch from including this link in its STP topology

PortFast immediately places the port in forwarding state bypassing STP listening and learning states; normally a port takes 30 seconds to enter forwarding; PortFast is appropriate for end devices that do not run STP and cannot cause loops; if configured on a port connected to another switch and BPDU guard is enabled the port shuts down (error-disabled) immediately when a BPDU is received; without BPDU guard a PortFast port on a switch-to-switch link can participate in creating loops

PortFast has no effect on ports connected to switches; when the switch detects a BPDU from a connected device it automatically disables PortFast and reverts to normal STP operation without any network impact

explanations.a: PortFast does not modify STP port priority; port priority is a separate parameter; PortFast only changes the timing of state transitions.
explanations.b: PortFast does not stop BPDU transmission; the switch continues generating and sending BPDUs on PortFast-configured ports; PortFast only changes the port state transition timing.
explanations.c: PortFast bypasses listening and learning states to provide immediate forwarding for end devices; this eliminates the 30-second wait for DHCP and network access; the risk when misapplied to switch ports is that a temporary loop could exist; BPDU guard is the companion feature that error-disables PortFast ports if BPDUs are received preventing loop formation when PortFast is accidentally applied to switch ports. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: PortFast does not automatically disable when BPDUs are received without BPDU guard; without BPDU guard the port remains in PortFast mode regardless of BPDU reception; only BPDU guard explicitly triggers error-disable when BPDUs arrive on a PortFast port.

questions.text: What is the maximum theoretical throughput of 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) and how does it differ from 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4)?

answers.text: 802.11ac provides theoretical maximum throughput up to 6.9 Gbps using multi-user MIMO, 256-QAM modulation, and channel widths up to 160 MHz exclusively in the 5 GHz band; 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4) provides up to 600 Mbps using up to 4 spatial streams, 64-QAM modulation, and 40 MHz channels in both 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz; 802.11ac added MU-MIMO (multi-user) while 802.11n used SU-MIMO (single-user)
802.11ac provides a maximum of 54 Mbps; 802.11n provides 11 Mbps; 802.11ac improved throughput by using the 5 GHz band exclusively instead of 2.4 GHz
802.11ac provides 300 Mbps maximum; 802.11n provides 150 Mbps; both operate in the 2.4 GHz band only; 802.11ac doubled the throughput by using two 2.4 GHz channels simultaneously
802.11ac and 802.11n are identical in throughput at 1.3 Gbps; 802.11ac simply added backward compatibility with older 802.11b/g devices which 802.11n did not support

explanations.a: 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) introduced key improvements over 802.11n: exclusive 5 GHz operation eliminating 2.4 GHz interference, wider channels (up to 160 MHz vs 40 MHz), higher-order modulation (256-QAM vs 64-QAM), up to 8 spatial streams, and MU-MIMO allowing simultaneous transmission to multiple clients; 802.11n was the first standard to introduce MIMO and dual-band operation. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: 54 Mbps is the maximum for 802.11a/g; 11 Mbps is the maximum for 802.11b; 802.11ac far exceeds these older standards.
explanations.c: Both standards support 5 GHz; 802.11n also supports 2.4 GHz; 802.11ac does not use two 2.4 GHz channels and does not operate in 2.4 GHz at all; the throughput figures stated are incorrect.
explanations.d: 802.11ac and 802.11n have significantly different throughputs; 802.11ac is not just a compatibility update; backward compatibility with 802.11a/b/g was present in 802.11n as well.

questions.text: A company is deploying Wi-Fi in a stadium with thousands of simultaneous users. The engineer recommends 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) over 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5). What specific 802.11ax feature addresses high-density environments that 802.11ac lacks?

answers.text: 802.11ax supports the 6 GHz band which 802.11ac does not; the additional spectrum provides more non-overlapping channels allowing each user to have a dedicated channel in a stadium environment

802.11ax uses beam steering to physically direct its antenna toward each user individually; in a stadium the AP rotates its antenna array to serve each of thousands of users sequentially at high speed making it appear simultaneous

802.11ax introduced OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access) which allows a single AP transmission to simultaneously serve multiple clients by dividing the channel into smaller resource units (RUs); 802.11ac used OFDM which dedicates the entire channel to one client per transmission; in high-density environments OFDMA dramatically improves efficiency by reducing medium contention and scheduling multiple clients in one time slot; 802.11ax also introduced BSS coloring to reduce co-channel interference from neighboring APs
802.11ax solves high-density problems by using shorter SSID names; shorter SSIDs consume fewer bytes in beacon frames reducing overhead and freeing airtime for user data in crowded environments

explanations.a: 802.11ax Wi-Fi 6 does not support 6 GHz; 6 GHz support came with Wi-Fi 6E (802.11ax extended) and Wi-Fi 7 (802.11be); the 6 GHz band is a valid improvement but it is not what distinguishes 802.11ax from 802.11ac for high-density.
explanations.b: Beamforming steers antenna gain toward specific clients but antennas do not physically rotate; beamforming was available in 802.11ac as well; it is not the feature that specifically addresses high-density multi-user efficiency.
explanations.c: OFDMA is the defining high-density improvement of 802.11ax; by subdividing channels into resource units multiple clients share a single transmission opportunity; this reduces the overhead of per-client channel contention which is the primary bottleneck in stadiums, airports, and conference halls; BSS coloring enables APs to ignore transmissions from overlapping BSSs on the same channel reducing unnecessary deferrals. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: SSID length has no meaningful impact on network throughput; SSID is included in beacon frames which represent negligible overhead; this is not a feature of any 802.11 standard.

questions.text: A legacy 802.11b device (11 Mbps) associates with an 802.11n access point. How does this affect other clients on the same AP and what mechanism causes this?

answers.text: The 802.11b device operates independently of 802.11n clients; modern APs isolate legacy clients on a separate radio so they cannot affect high-speed clients

The 802.11b device upgrades to 802.11n automatically when it associates with an 802.11n AP; the AP provides a firmware-in-the-air update that upgrades the legacy device's wireless capabilities to match the AP standard
The 802.11b device only affects its own connection speed; other 802.11n clients continue at full 802.11n rates because the AP manages separate queues for legacy and modern clients

The 802.11b device forces all transmissions to use 802.11b-compatible rates; when the legacy client is present the AP must send protection frames (RTS/CTS or CTS-to-self) before every transmission to prevent collisions with the legacy client; these protection frames use 802.11b rates (1-11 Mbps) and consume significant airtime degrading throughput for all clients on the same channel by 50% or more

explanations.a: Most APs do not have completely isolated radios for legacy clients; even if dual-radio the 2.4 GHz radio handles all clients including legacy ones; one legacy client on the same channel affects all clients on that channel.
explanations.b: APs cannot upgrade client device firmware over the air; wireless drivers and hardware capabilities are determined by the client hardware and its own firmware; the AP standard does not change the client's capabilities.
explanations.c: The AP cannot isolate 802.11n transmissions from interference by 802.11b clients on the same channel; the shared wireless medium means protection is required for all transmissions when any legacy client is associated.
explanations.d: Legacy protection is a real performance problem; 802.11n mixed mode requires CTS-to-self or RTS/CTS before each data frame when legacy clients are present; these protection frames use old slow rates consuming airtime; this is why enterprise deployments often disable 802.11b support entirely to improve overall network performance. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: An enterprise wireless network uses 802.11ax with WPA3-Enterprise. A security team requests target wake time (TWT) be enabled for IoT sensors. What is TWT and what benefit does it provide specifically for IoT deployments?

answers.text: TWT (Target Wake Time) is a WPA3 security feature that schedules when devices are allowed to transmit; it prevents IoT devices from sending data during scheduled security scans protecting the network from IoT-based attacks

TWT is an 802.11ax power management feature where the AP and client negotiate specific scheduled wake intervals; IoT sensors that only report data periodically (temperature, humidity, occupancy) can remain in deep sleep between scheduled transmission windows rather than constantly listening for beacon frames; this dramatically extends battery life for battery-powered IoT sensors (from months to years) while reducing medium contention since sleeping devices do not compete for channel access between their scheduled slots

TWT (Target Wake Time) is a feature that wakes sleeping clients when their buffered frames reach a target size threshold; IoT devices benefit because they receive larger batched data deliveries reducing the frequency of radio activation

TWT is a QoS feature that assigns time slots to different traffic classes; IoT traffic receives dedicated time slots preventing it from competing with voice and video traffic; this is analogous to TDMA cellular scheduling applied to Wi-Fi

explanations.a: TWT is not a security feature; it is a power management and scheduling feature defined in 802.11ax; WPA3 provides the security framework separately.
explanations.b: TWT (IEEE 802.11ax) enables AP-negotiated wake schedules for clients; a temperature sensor that reports every 5 minutes can sleep for 4 minutes 59 seconds waking only briefly to transmit and receive acknowledgment; this reduces battery consumption by orders of magnitude compared to legacy power saving mechanisms; it also reduces channel contention since sleeping devices are not competing for access during their sleep windows. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: TWT schedules specific wake times not data size thresholds; the 'target' in the scheduled time to wake not a data quantity target; this description does not match TWT's actual mechanism.
explanations.d: TWT is a power management scheduling feature not a QoS traffic class feature; it schedules when individual devices wake up not which traffic class gets priority; QoS in Wi-Fi is handled by WMM (Wi-Fi Multimedia) and EDCA mechanisms.

questions.text: A user's laptop frequently drops its wireless connection while devices on the other side of the office maintain stable connections. The office has many microwave ovens and cordless phones. Which frequency band is most likely experiencing interference and why?

answers.text: The 2.4 GHz band is experiencing interference; microwave ovens operate at approximately 2.45 GHz and older cordless phones use 2.4 GHz; these devices generate RF noise that directly overlaps with the 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi channels; the 2.4 GHz band has only 3 non-overlapping channels in North America (1, 6, 11) making congestion and interference more severe than the 5 GHz band which has more channels and fewer competing devices
The 5 GHz band is experiencing interference; microwave ovens and cordless phones primarily operate at 5 GHz because it provides more power for cooking and voice transmission
The 6 GHz band is experiencing interference; modern appliances and phones operate at 6 GHz; the laptop's connection drops because it is trying to use 6 GHz Wi-Fi 6E which is blocked by these devices

Neither band is affected by microwave ovens or cordless phones; these devices use proprietary RF shielding that prevents any emissions from reaching Wi-Fi frequencies; the user's connection drops are caused by driver software issues

explanations.a: 2.4 GHz congestion and interference is a well-documented problem in office environments; the ISM band at 2.4 GHz is shared with microwave ovens (2.45 GHz), older cordless phones, Bluetooth, baby monitors, and ZigBee devices; the narrow band with only 3 non-overlapping channels creates significant co-channel interference; switching to 5 GHz or 6 GHz eliminates most of this interference. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: Microwave ovens and common cordless phones primarily operate at 2.4 GHz not 5 GHz; microwave ovens use 2.45 GHz for their heating frequency which directly overlaps with 802.11b/g/n channels.
explanations.c: 6 GHz is not used by microwave ovens or standard cordless phones; 6 GHz Wi-Fi (Wi-Fi 6E) is a relatively new technology; common office appliances do not operate at 6 GHz.
explanations.d: Microwave ovens are well-documented sources of 2.4 GHz interference; they do not have RF shielding that prevents external emissions; their magnetrons emit broadband RF noise in the 2.4 GHz range that affects nearby Wi-Fi devices.

questions.text: An engineer designs a wireless network for a 20-story office building. They notice that devices on upper floors can see APs from other floors causing co-channel interference. The 2.4 GHz deployment has severe performance issues. How does deploying 5 GHz improve this situation?

answers.text: 5 GHz radio waves cannot travel vertically; they propagate only horizontally so upper-floor APs cannot interfere with lower-floor APs regardless of channel assignment

5 GHz requires users to stand closer to the AP reducing the range of each AP to less than 10 meters; this limited range prevents signals from reaching other floors eliminating the co-channel interference problem

5 GHz provides more non-overlapping channels (up to 25 in the US using 20 MHz channels) compared to 2.4 GHz (only 3 non-overlapping channels 1, 6, 11); with more channels the engineer can assign different channels to vertically adjacent APs reducing co-channel interference; additionally 5 GHz signals have higher free-space path loss attenuating more quickly through floors and walls further limiting inter-floor interference

5 GHz uses a different modulation scheme (QAM vs OFDM) that is physically blocked by concrete floors; 2.4 GHz OFDM penetrates concrete while 5 GHz QAM does not; deploying 5 GHz isolates each floor automatically

explanations.a: 5 GHz radio waves propagate in all directions including vertically; they are not physically restricted to horizontal propagation; floor-to-floor interference is reduced by higher path loss and channel availability not by directional propagation restriction.
explanations.b: 5 GHz does have shorter range than 2.4 GHz for equal power due to higher path loss but the range is typically 15-30 meters indoors not less than 10 meters; the reduced range is a consequence of physics not a design choice requiring users to stand close.
explanations.c: Channel availability and path loss are the two key advantages of 5 GHz in multi-story buildings; with 25 available 20 MHz channels the engineer can implement a channel reuse pattern that avoids co-channel assignments between vertically adjacent APs; 5 GHz signals experience greater attenuation through building materials naturally reducing the interference radius of each AP. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: Both 5 GHz and 2.4 GHz use OFDM modulation; modulation scheme does not determine whether signals penetrate concrete; 5 GHz experiences greater attenuation through materials due to higher frequency (shorter wavelength) not due to modulation scheme.

questions.text: Wi-Fi 6E introduced the 6 GHz band. A network architect evaluates 6 GHz deployment for a new hospital wing. What are the key characteristics of 6 GHz that make it advantageous, and what limitations must be considered?

answers.text: 6 GHz is advantageous because it supports longer range than 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz; Wi-Fi signals at 6 GHz can penetrate walls more effectively allowing fewer APs to cover the hospital wing; the limitation is that 6 GHz requires a special license from the FCC costing approximately $50,000 per facility

6 GHz is unlicensed but restricted to outdoor use only; indoor deployment of 6 GHz Wi-Fi is prohibited by FCC regulations; hospitals must use 5 GHz for indoor coverage and can optionally add 6 GHz for outdoor campus areas

6 GHz is identical to 5 GHz in all technical characteristics; the only difference is regulatory designation; the architect should choose based on which band the hospital's existing APs advertise first in their beacon frames

6 GHz provides a clean uncongested spectrum (1200 MHz of bandwidth in the US supporting up to 59 non-overlapping 20 MHz channels or 7 non-overlapping 160 MHz channels) with no legacy device interference since only Wi-Fi 6E and newer devices use it; advantages include very high throughput with wide channels, low latency, and freedom from 2.4/5 GHz congestion; limitations include shorter range than 2.4 GHz due to higher path loss, reduced penetration through walls (important in a building with thick hospital walls), and the requirement for 6 GHz-capable client devices which may not include all medical devices in the hospital

explanations.a: 6 GHz has shorter range than 2.4 GHz and comparable or shorter range than 5 GHz due to higher free-space path loss; it does not penetrate walls more effectively; no special FCC license is required for Wi-Fi 6E; the 6 GHz band is unlicensed for indoor use under FCC part 15.
explanations.b: FCC rules (Part 15.407) allow indoor use of 6 GHz Wi-Fi under standard power limits; outdoor use has additional power restrictions; indoor 6 GHz Wi-Fi deployment is explicitly permitted and is the primary intended use case for Wi-Fi 6E.
explanations.c: 6 GHz and 5 GHz have different technical characteristics including frequency, available channels, path loss, and device requirements; they are not identical; the frequency difference produces meaningfully different propagation and interference characteristics.
explanations.d: 6 GHz offers massive spectrum expansion with a clean slate free from legacy interference; the 1200 MHz bandwidth in the US (5925-7125 MHz) dwarfs both 2.4 GHz (83.5 MHz) and 5 GHz (500 MHz); 160 MHz channels enable multi-gigabit throughput; the higher frequency means faster attenuation through walls which matters in a hospital with thick construction; medical devices and equipment must support Wi-Fi 6E to use 6 GHz. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: A wireless engineer uses a spectrum analyzer and finds the 2.4 GHz band is heavily congested with neighbor APs all on channels 1, 6, and 11. A neighbor AP is using channel 3. What problem does the channel 3 AP create and what is the recommended solution?

answers.text: Channel 3 is a valid non-overlapping channel in the 2.4 GHz band; channels 1, 3, and 11 are the three non-overlapping channels in North America; the engineer should configure their AP to use channel 3 as well to match the environment

Channel 3 causes adjacent channel interference with both channel 1 (overlap) and channel 6 (overlap); in 802.11b/g/n the 2.4 GHz channels are 5 MHz apart but each channel uses 22 MHz of bandwidth; channel 3 overlaps with channels 1 through 5 and with channels 1-5; the recommended solution is to use only the three non-overlapping channels (1, 6, 11 in North America) and educate neighboring organizations about proper channel planning; the engineer should also consider moving their network to 5 GHz or 6 GHz to avoid 2.4 GHz congestion entirely

Channel 3 is a hidden channel used by legacy 802.11b equipment that modern APs cannot detect; it does not cause interference with channels 1, 6, or 11 but does interfere with Bluetooth devices operating on channel 3

Channel 3 creates no interference problem; in 2.4 GHz the channels are completely independent frequency allocations with no overlap; any channel from 1-11 can be used without affecting adjacent channels

explanations.a: Channels 1, 3, and 11 are NOT the three non-overlapping channels; the correct non-overlapping channels in North America are 1, 6, and 11 which are separated by 5 channels (25 MHz) to avoid overlap; channel 3 overlaps with both channel 1 and channel 6.
explanations.b: Adjacent channel interference from channel 3 degrades performance on channels 1 and 6 simultaneously; 2.4 GHz channels are 5 MHz apart but 802.11 channels use 22 MHz of bandwidth; a channel 3 transmission partially occupies the spectrum of channels 1 through 5 causing interference without proper CSMA/CD detection; the APs on channel 3 neither properly defer to channel 1 nor channel 6 APs creating a worst-case interference scenario. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Channel 3 is detectable by modern APs through spectrum analysis; it is not a hidden channel; Bluetooth uses frequency hopping across the entire 2.4 GHz band not a specific 802.11 channel.
explanations.d: 2.4 GHz channels overlap significantly; channels are not independent frequency allocations; 802.11 channels 1 through 11 are staggered by only 5 MHz while requiring 22 MHz of bandwidth; all channels except 1, 6, and 11 overlap with their neighbors.

questions.text: What is channel bonding in 802.11n/ac wireless and what is the trade-off?

answers.text: Channel bonding combines two or more adjacent 20 MHz channels into a wider 40, 80, or 160 MHz channel to increase throughput; a 40 MHz channel provides approximately double the throughput of a 20 MHz channel; the trade-off is reduced channel availability (a 40 MHz channel consumes two of the three non-overlapping 2.4 GHz channels) and increased likelihood of interference in dense environments where multiple APs compete for the same wider spectrum
Channel bonding combines two AP radios into a single logical radio doubling the transmit power; the trade-off is reduced range because higher power causes interference for nearby APs
Channel bonding links two separate APs together over a wired backhaul so they appear as a single AP to clients; the trade-off is that losing one AP removes the bonded pair from service

Channel bonding combines 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz channels into a single logical channel; clients use both bands simultaneously doubling throughput; the trade-off is that only dual-band clients can benefit from the bonded channel

explanations.a: Channel bonding (also called channel aggregation) merges adjacent 20 MHz channels into wider channels; 802.11n introduced 40 MHz bonding; 802.11ac added 80 MHz and 160 MHz; wider channels increase throughput linearly but consume more spectrum; in 2.4 GHz a 40 MHz channel uses 2 of the 3 non-overlapping channels making it practical only in low-density environments. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: Channel bonding does not combine radios or increase transmit power; it refers to combining frequency channels to create a wider frequency allocation for higher data rates.
explanations.c: Channel bonding is a radio frequency concept not a wired backhaul feature; connecting two APs via wired backhaul is called a wireless distribution system or meshing and is a separate concept.
explanations.d: Channel bonding combines channels within the same frequency band not across different bands; simultaneous dual-band operation for one client is a different feature called band steering or in 802.11be (Wi-Fi 7) multi-link operation.

questions.text: A wireless engineer deploys 802.11ac APs using 80 MHz channels in the 5 GHz band. Users in a conference room with many people experience poor performance. The engineer considers switching to 20 MHz channels. How would this change improve performance?

answers.text: Switching to 20 MHz channels would reduce performance further; wider channels always provide better performance regardless of density; the conference room problem is caused by something other than channel width

Switching to 20 MHz channels increases each client's individual throughput by reducing the overhead of wider channels; each 80 MHz frame contains 4 times the overhead of a 20 MHz frame making narrow channels more efficient per bit

Switching to 20 MHz channels reduces the amount of spectrum each client occupies allowing more non-overlapping channels; in a high-density environment with many clients competing for the same channel narrower channels mean more APs can operate on non-overlapping channels nearby; a single 80 MHz channel in the 5 GHz band occupies the same spectrum as four 20 MHz channels; having four separate 20 MHz APs each serving fewer clients can outperform one 80 MHz AP serving all clients due to reduced contention per AP

20 MHz channels provide stronger signal penetration through conference room walls; the 80 MHz signal scatters when passing through humans in the conference room while 20 MHz passes through unimpeded due to its longer wavelength

explanations.a: Wider channels provide higher throughput per client but reduce the total number of available non-overlapping channels; in high-density environments the reduced channel count creates more competition between clients and interference between APs; narrower channels can improve aggregate performance.
explanations.b: Wider channels provide more subcarriers per frame reducing relative overhead not increasing it; 80 MHz channels are more spectral efficient per bit than 20 MHz channels for individual clients; the advantage of 20 MHz in high density is channel reuse not individual frame efficiency.
explanations.c: Channel reuse and contention reduction are the key factors in high-density wireless; the 5 GHz band supports 24 non-overlapping 20 MHz channels but only 6 non-overlapping 80 MHz channels; in a conference room with 50 users multiple narrower-channel APs serving 12-15 users each outperform one wide-channel AP serving all 50 because each AP has fewer clients competing for access. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: Channel width does not significantly affect wall penetration; the frequency (2.4 vs 5 GHz) determines penetration behavior much more than channel width within the same band; human bodies do attenuate 5 GHz signals but this is a frequency characteristic not a channel width characteristic.

questions.text: An 802.11ac deployment uses 80 MHz channels. The engineer reports DFS (Dynamic Frequency Selection) is causing periodic AP outages. What is DFS and why does it cause AP service interruptions?

answers.text: DFS (Dynamic Frequency Selection) is a Wi-Fi security mechanism that rotates channels randomly every 30 minutes to prevent unauthorized monitoring of wireless traffic; the interruptions occur while clients reconnect to the new channel

DFS only applies to outdoor wireless deployments; indoor 802.11ac APs are exempt from DFS requirements; the engineer's indoor AP is likely misconfigured to use DFS channels when it should use non-DFS channels automatically

DFS is an 802.11ac feature that dynamically selects the fastest available channel based on traffic load; when the AP switches to a faster channel clients briefly disconnect; the outages correlate with periods of heavy traffic when DFS seeks a less congested channel

DFS is an FCC/regulatory requirement for Wi-Fi devices using certain 5 GHz channels to detect and avoid interference with radar systems (weather radar, military radar, TDWR); when an AP detects a radar signal on its current channel it must immediately vacate that channel and switch to a non-radar channel; clients disconnect during channel change and must reassociate; wider channels (80 MHz) are more vulnerable to DFS events because more spectrum means more radar channels are included in the AP's operating range

explanations.a: DFS is not a security mechanism for channel rotation; it is a regulatory requirement for spectrum sharing with radar systems; security channel rotation is a separate concept not related to DFS.
explanations.b: DFS applies to both indoor and outdoor Wi-Fi deployments using DFS channels (52-144) in the 5 GHz band; indoor APs are not exempt from DFS requirements; in practice weather radar and TDWR transmitters can trigger indoor AP DFS events depending on proximity.
explanations.c: DFS is a regulatory radar-avoidance mechanism not a performance optimization feature; channel selection based on load is a separate feature called auto-channel or band steering; DFS events are triggered by radar detection not traffic conditions.
explanations.d: DFS is mandated by FCC (and international regulators) to prevent Wi-Fi from interfering with radar systems that share 5 GHz spectrum; channels 52-144 in the 5 GHz band are DFS channels; an 80 MHz channel centered in this range may span multiple DFS channels; when radar pulses are detected the AP must vacate within 10 seconds and cannot return for 30 minutes; during the channel change all clients disconnect and must reassociate which causes the service interruption. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: What is the difference between an SSID, a BSSID, a BSS, and an ESS?

answers.text: SSID is the device name of the AP hardware; BSSID is the company name of the AP manufacturer; BSS is a single AP and all its clients; ESS is a group of APs from the same manufacturer sharing roaming agreements

SSID (Service Set Identifier) is the human-readable network name such as CorpWiFi; BSSID (Basic Service Set Identifier) is the MAC address of a specific AP radio that uniquely identifies it; a BSS (Basic Service Set) is one AP and all clients associated to it forming a single cell; an ESS (Extended Service Set) is multiple BSSs (APs) sharing the same SSID connected by a common distribution system allowing client roaming between APs while maintaining the same network name

SSID is the IP subnet used by wireless clients; BSSID is the broadcast address of the wireless subnet; BSS is the routing domain for wireless traffic; ESS is the extended IP subnet created when multiple APs share the same SSID
SSID, BSSID, BSS, and ESS are all different names for the same concept of a wireless network; the terms are used interchangeably in different IEEE documents and vendor implementations

explanations.a: These definitions are incorrect; SSID is the network name not device name; BSSID is the AP radio MAC address not manufacturer name; ESS is about multiple APs sharing the same SSID not manufacturer grouping.
explanations.b: These four terms have distinct meanings in IEEE 802.11: the SSID is what users see when scanning for networks; the BSSID uniquely identifies each AP radio (multiple radios on one AP have different BSSIDs); the BSS is the fundamental wireless cell consisting of one AP and its clients; the ESS extends this to enterprise networks where multiple APs share one SSID and clients roam seamlessly. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: None of these terms refer to IP subnets or routing domains; they are all Layer 2 wireless network identifiers; IP addressing is independent of the BSS/ESS structure.
explanations.d: These four terms have distinct and different meanings; they are not interchangeable; each describes a different aspect of wireless network architecture.

questions.text: A user's laptop shows two networks with the same SSID 'CorpNet' but different signal strengths. Their laptop connected to the weaker signal and performance is poor. What wireless behavior is occurring and how should it be addressed?

answers.text: The user's laptop is exhibiting sticky client behavior; clients determine when to roam and many Windows and device drivers hold connections to the original AP even when a stronger AP is available; the laptop connected to the weaker BSSID (specific AP) and is unwilling to roam to the stronger one; solutions include: configuring minimum RSSI thresholds on APs to force client disassociation from weak signals (aggressive roaming), enabling 802.11r/k/v (fast BSS transition, radio resource management, BSS transition management) to provide roaming assistance to clients, or using a wireless controller with client steering functionality
The two networks cannot have the same SSID; the laptop must be experiencing a network naming conflict; both APs must be renamed to unique SSIDs to resolve the confusion

The two CorpNet SSIDs are from different organizations; the user accidentally connected to a neighboring company's guest network; the laptop should be forced to forget the neighboring network and use certificate-based 802.1X authentication to ensure it always connects to the correct CorpNet

The two CorpNet networks are the same AP broadcasting on two different frequencies; the client always connects to the 2.4 GHz version which is weaker; disabling 2.4 GHz on the AP forces the client to use 5 GHz

explanations.a: Sticky client behavior is a pervasive enterprise Wi-Fi problem; the 802.11 standard gives clients full control over roaming decisions; devices often remain associated to the original AP until the signal is completely unusable rather than proactively roaming; 802.11v BSS Transition Management allows APs to suggest clients move to a better AP; 802.11k provides neighbor reports so clients know which APs are nearby; 802.11r enables fast handoff once the client decides to roam. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: Multiple APs with the same SSID is the standard enterprise wireless design (ESS); the SSID is shared across all APs intentionally so clients roam between them; different BSSIDs (AP MAC addresses) distinguish the individual APs while the SSID remains common.
explanations.c: Same-SSID networks from neighboring organizations would have different BSSIDs and different security configurations; if the network uses 802.1X the certificate would prevent connecting to an imposter network; sticky client on the corporate ESS is the more likely explanation.
explanations.d: An AP broadcasting the same SSID on both bands is a single BSS per radio; the user would see two entries with the same SSID but different BSSIDs for the 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz radios; this could be the cause but sticky client behavior is the more general and likely explanation for the described scenario.

questions.text: An enterprise wireless controller shows a rogue AP with the same SSID as the corporate network but a different BSSID. Users are inadvertently connecting to it. What is this attack called and what detection/prevention mechanisms address it?

answers.text: This is a WPS brute force attack; the rogue AP is using WPS PIN vulnerability to capture corporate SSID credentials; disabling WPS on all corporate APs prevents the attack

This is a VLAN hopping attack adapted for wireless; the rogue AP uses a different BSSID to place clients in an unprotected VLAN; the solution is configuring wireless VLANs to require matching BSSIDs before VLAN assignment

This is an evil twin or honeypot attack; the attacker broadcasts the same SSID as the corporate network to lure users; since clients trust the SSID name they connect to the strongest signal regardless of BSSID; detection mechanisms include: wireless IDS/IPS that correlates BSSIDs against known-legitimate APs and alerts on unrecognized BSSIDs using corporate SSIDs; prevention includes 802.1X authentication (users cannot authenticate to the evil twin if the authentication server rejects it), client certificate validation of the authentication server certificate preventing credential theft, and containment where the wireless controller sends deauthentication frames to clients connected to the rogue BSSID

This is normal ESS roaming behavior; multiple BSSIDs with the same SSID is how enterprise roaming works; the user connecting to a different BSSID means they roamed to a new AP which is the intended ESS design
